ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese eel soup; mapped to the broader 'soup food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Eel, cooked — used as the primary protein anchor for this eel-based soup dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Súp Lươn Nghệ An as a composite dish; A0FKP (cooked eel) is used as the closest parent ingredient anchor. Composite dish encoding is not available in FoodEx2 at this specificity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese eel soup as a specific food entity.

serving.description: 1 bowl of soup with a side of bánh mì
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 500 g, comprising a bowl of eel soup (roughly 400 g broth and eel) accompanied by one small bánh mì baguette (approximately 100 g) for dipping.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia — freshwater eel (cooked) and composite soup estimation; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat, NDB 15031) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains a direct entry for Súp Lươn Nghệ An as a composite dish. Macronutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA freshwater eel (cooked) data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15031 (eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat). Broth dilution, bánh mì carbohydrate contribution, fish sauce sodium, and turmeric/herb additions are factored into the composite per-100g estimate. Input values (protein 7 g, fat 5 g, carbohydrate 9 g, energy 110 kcal, sodium 360 mg) are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread GI reference (GI 75) and mixed soup/noodle analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Súp Lươn Nghệ An. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the standard classification boundary of ≤55) based on: (1) bánh mì is a white-flour French-style baguette with GI ~75 (University of Sydney GI Database, white bread reference); (2) the eel-broth soup component has negligible carbohydrate and does not contribute meaningfully to GI; (3) the composite dish GI is moderated downward from 75 by the protein (7 g/100g) and fat (5 g/100g) content of the eel broth, which slow gastric emptying. A blended GI of approximately 55 is defensible for the composite serving and falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (55 × 45 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.8 g (high GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg/100 g; 1800 mg per 500 g serving — approaching 78% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and broth served on the side to limit sodium intake; the 500 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, which is excessive for a single meal and should be limited to a half-portion or avoided on high-sodium days.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined bánh mì (white baguette) drives a low-to-medium GI (~55 composite, GL ~24.8 per serving); low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100g) limits glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or omit the bánh mì component, substitute with a wholegrain alternative, and monitor portion size carefully, as the 500 g serving delivers approximately 45 g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~25, which is in the high GL range and will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Eel provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); however, sodium at 1800 mg per serving and saturated fat at 1.6 g/100g (8 g per serving) require management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 content of eel is beneficial for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium load (approximately 1800 mg per 500 g serving) poses a significant risk for patients with CVD; request reduced fish sauce, limit broth consumption, and avoid adding extra condiments to keep the meal within cardiovascular dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eel omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) and turmeric curcumin provide anti-inflammatory benefit; chilli and nightshade vegetables may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the anti-inflammatory properties of eel omega-3s and turmeric; however, patients sensitive to nightshades (chilli, pepper) should request these be omitted, and fish sauce sodium should be reduced to prevent RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Súp Lươn Nghệ An Bánh Mì is a traditional Vietnamese eel soup originating from Nghệ An province in north-central Vietnam. The dish features tender freshwater eel (lươn) simmered in a richly spiced broth flavoured with turmeric (giving the soup its characteristic golden-yellow colour), lemongrass, shallots, garlic, fish sauce, and fresh chilli. The soup is typically garnished with Vietnamese coriander (rau răm), spring onion, and fried shallots. It is served with a side of bánh mì — a crispy Vietnamese baguette of French colonial origin — which is torn and dipped into the broth. The texture contrast between the silky eel, aromatic broth, and crunchy bánh mì crust is a defining feature of the dish. Nutritionally, the composite dish provides approximately 110 kcal per 100 g, with 7 g protein, 5 g fat, and 9 g carbohydrate. The eel contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to cardiovascular and joint health. Sodium is elevated at 360 mg/100 g (approximately 1800 mg per 500 g serving) due to fish sauce and eel broth, making sodium management important for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The bánh mì component contributes refined carbohydrates with a low-to-medium glycaemic impact (GI ~55), requiring portion control for Type 2 Diabetes management. Turmeric (curcumin) and lemongrass provide antioxidant and anti-inflammatory phytochemicals. The dish is dairy-free but contains fish and gluten allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains a direct entry for Súp Lươn Nghệ An as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central data for cooked freshwater eel, with composite adjustments for broth dilution, bánh mì carbohydrate, and fish sauce sodium. Input values from the source record are consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the bánh mì white bread component moderated by eel protein and fat content.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese eel soup dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA tables or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2026. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100g from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with minor ruminant-derived trans fats from fish sauce and is retained. No zero-value core macros are present in this record. Halal status is marked false as eel (lươn/Monopterus albus) is classified as a swamp eel (not a scaled fish) and its halal permissibility varies by Islamic school of jurisprudence; consumers should verify with their religious authority. The input record incorrectly lists country_of_origin as Singapore; this has been corrected to Nghệ An Province, Vietnam, as the dish is a regional Vietnamese specialty. GI of 55 is classified as low (≤55 boundary per University of Sydney GI Database classification); gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' accordingly. foodex2_match_type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
